item.title: Agfamatic 2008
item.description:
The Agfamatic 2008 is my go-to camera for spontaneous moments. Its pocketable size makes it easy to carry everywhere, and the 110 film format gives a unique, nostalgic look to the shots.

Why I Love It

  • Ultra-compact, drop-in cartridge loading
  • Simple point-and-shoot operation
  • Charming 70s design and vintage aesthetic
  • Perfect for street photography and travel

Tips for Using It

  1. Use higher ISO 110 film for better results in low light
  2. Get close to your subjects - the fixed focal length works best at close range
  3. Embrace the grain and softness - that’s part of the charm
  4. Keep the viewfinder and lens clean for the sharpest results

item.title: Canon AE-1 Program
item.description: The Canon AE-1 Program was my first film camera, and it’s been a reliable companion in my photography journey. Its program mode makes it beginner-friendly while still offering full manual control when needed.

item.title: Gallery
item.description:
Film Gallery

Coming soon: A curated collection of my favorite film shots

Categories

  • Street Photography
  • Portraits
  • Landscapes
  • Still Life
  • Experimental

item.title: Learnings
item.description:
Film Photography Learnings

Here are some key lessons I’ve learned along my film photography journey. I’ll continue to update this page as I learn more.

Technical Learnings

Exposure

  • Always bracket your shots when in doubt
  • The light meter in vintage cameras can be fooled by bright backgrounds
  • Negative film has more latitude than slide film
  • Overexposing slightly is often better than underexposing

Development

  • Temperature control is crucial for consistent results
  • Agitation technique affects grain and contrast
  • Fresh chemicals make a big difference
  • Keep detailed notes for each roll

Creative Learnings

Composition

  • The square format of medium format cameras forces you to think differently
  • Slowing down leads to better compositions
  • Sometimes the best shot is the one you didn’t take
  • Look for leading lines and patterns

Film Choice

  • Different films have different personalities
  • Black and white film is great for learning
  • Color film can be expensive, so make each shot count
  • Expired film can yield interesting results

Practical Tips

Equipment

  • Keep your cameras clean and well-maintained
  • Always carry extra batteries
  • A light meter app on your phone is a great backup
  • Invest in a good scanner or find a reliable lab

Workflow

  • Label your film rolls immediately after shooting
  • Keep a detailed shooting log
  • Organize your negatives carefully
  • Back up your scans in multiple places

Resources I’ve Found Helpful

  • The Film Photography Project
  • r/analog on Reddit
  • The Darkroom for development and scanning
  • Emulsive for film reviews and tutorials

item.title: Learnings
item.description:
Film Learnings

Getting Started

  1. Start Simple: Begin with a basic 35mm camera like the Canon AE-1. It’s user-friendly but still teaches you the fundamentals.
  2. Learn the Basics: Understand exposure (aperture, shutter speed, ISO) before moving to more advanced techniques.
  3. Practice Loading Film: Practice loading and unloading film in daylight until you’re comfortable doing it in the dark.

Development Process

Black and White Development

  • Use Ilford ID-11 developer at 1:1 dilution
  • Standard development time: 7-8 minutes at 20°C
  • Always use fresh fixer
  • Wash thoroughly after fixing

Tips for Better Results

  1. Meter Carefully: Take your time with light metering. It’s better to bracket exposures when unsure.
  2. Keep Notes: Document your settings and development times for each roll.
  3. Handle with Care: Always handle film by the edges and keep it away from heat.
  4. Be Patient: Film photography is a slow process. Embrace it.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

  1. Not checking the film rewind knob before opening the camera
  2. Forgetting to set the correct ISO
  3. Rushing the development process
  4. Not cleaning the camera regularly

Resources

  • The Darkroom - Great for film developing and scanning
  • r/analog - Reddit community for film photography
  • Film Photography Project - Educational resources and film reviews

item.title: Pentax SP2
item.description:
The Pentax SP2 is my main 35mm camera for more serious photography. It’s a robust and reliable SLR with excellent build quality and a wide range of compatible lenses.

Why I Love It

  • Solid metal construction
  • Bright viewfinder
  • Accurate light meter
  • Compatible with many M42 mount lenses
  • Simple, intuitive controls
  • Classic 70s design

My Experience

I found this camera at a local camera shop in excellent condition. It came with a 50mm f/1.8 Takumar lens, which is incredibly sharp. The camera has never let me down, and the light meter is surprisingly accurate for its age. The M42 mount gives me access to a wide range of affordable, high-quality lenses.

item.title: Rollei B35
item.description:
The Rollei B35 is one of the smallest full-frame 35mm cameras ever made, and it still feels incredibly well built for its size. No rangefinder, just zone focusing and a built-in light meter - it forces you to slow down and guess distances, which is part of the fun.

Why I Love It

  • Genuinely pocketable, solid German engineering
  • Sharp little Triotar lens
  • Great for quick, unobtrusive street shots
  • Looks as good as it shoots

Tips for Using It

  1. Practice zone focusing by pacing off distances until it becomes second nature
  2. Works best in good light given the fixed f/3.5 lens
  3. Keep the selenium meter out of direct sun when metering for accurate readings
  4. A wrist strap helps since there’s no grip to speak of

item.title: Yashica Mat-124G
item.description: The Yashica Mat-124G is my medium format camera of choice. Its square format and waist-level viewfinder offer a unique perspective, and the image quality from its Yashinon lens is exceptional.

item.title: Head of DevSecOps
item.description: Leading DevSecOps initiatives and infrastructure development for a self-service platform serving policyholders in the independent insurance channel. Focusing on security, automation, and scalable infrastructure.

item.title: Senior System Software Engineer
item.description: Systems & Web Development on a self-service platform for policyholders inside the independent insurance channel.

item.title: About Me
item.description:
Hey there!

My name is Seth Moeckel, and I really enjoy designing & building digital products. I was about 12 years old when I started messing with computers. I really didn’t understand much, but I wanted to know how things worked, and how I could make my ideas real. I started taking appliances apart, building robots with microcontrollers, and writing small scripts for my Raspberry Pi. It took a while but I began to grasp programming, and how to build my own programs.

item.title: Senior Software Engineer
item.description: Web Development on a game-changing lifestyle apartments company built on the combination of long term leasing, tenant-driven home sharing and furnished hotel stays.

item.title: Full Stack Software Engineer (Contactor)
item.description: Led Software Architecture and Backend Development for an amazing peer-2-peer content rating and recommendations platform. Later advised and helped drive hiring processes, as well as managing a highly distributed team of international developers.

item.title: Partner, Consultant
item.description: Led Software Architecture, Mobile Design & Development, DevOps and Backend Development for the new relaunch of a premier gig-economy solution for facilities management.

item.title: Full Stack Software Engineer
item.description: Distributed Systems, Windows Systems and Web Development on the world’s first AI-as-a-service for healthcare.

item.title: Founder, Freelance Developer
item.description: Founded a freelance development studio, delivering web and mobile applications for small business clients from concept through launch.

item.title: Fullstack Software Engineer (Contractor)
item.description: Contract fullstack engineering work on RapChat’s web and backend platform, supporting a mobile app for recording and sharing rap songs.

item.title: Software Engineering Fellow
item.description: Software engineering fellowship focused on backend development and healthcare data automation, working alongside a senior engineering team to ship production features.
